title: 16.3 — std::vector and the unsigned length and subscript problem

content.text: Related contentWe cover nested typedefs in lesson15.3 -- Nested types (member types).
We cover nested typedefs in lesson15.3 -- Nested types (member types).

Key insightsize_typeis a nested typedef defined in standard library container classes, used as the type for the length (and indices, if supported) of the container class.size_typedefaults tostd::size_t, and since this is almost never changed, we can reasonably assumesize_typeis an alias forstd::size_t.

size_typeis a nested typedef defined in standard library container classes, used as the type for the length (and indices, if supported) of the container class.size_typedefaults tostd::size_t, and since this is almost never changed, we can reasonably assumesize_typeis an alias forstd::size_t.
size_typedefaults tostd::size_t, and since this is almost never changed, we can reasonably assumesize_typeis an alias forstd::size_t.

For advanced readersAll of the standard library containers exceptstd::arrayusestd::allocatorto allocate memory. For these containers,T::size_typeis derived from thesize_typeof the allocator used. Sincestd::allocatorcan allocate up tostd::size_tbytes of memory,std::allocator<T>::size_typeis defined asstd::size_t. Therefore,T::size_typedefaults tostd::size_t.Only in cases where a custom allocator whoseT::size_typeis defined as something other thanstd::size_twill a container’sT::size_typebe something other thanstd::size_t. This is rare and something done on a per-application basis, so it’s generally safe to assumeT::size_typewill bestd::size_tunless your application is using such a custom allocator (and you will know if that is the case).

For advanced readersBecausestd::size()can also be used on non-decayed C-style arrays, this method is sometimes favored over the using thesize()member function (particularly when writing function templates that can accept either a container class or non-decayed C-style array argument).We discuss C-style array decay in lesson17.8 -- C-style array decay.

Becausestd::size()can also be used on non-decayed C-style arrays, this method is sometimes favored over the using thesize()member function (particularly when writing function templates that can accept either a container class or non-decayed C-style array argument).We discuss C-style array decay in lesson17.8 -- C-style array decay.
We discuss C-style array decay in lesson17.8 -- C-style array decay.

For advanced readersWhen theat()member function encounters an out-of-bounds index, it actually throws an exception of typestd::out_of_range. If the exception is not handled, the program will be terminated. We cover exceptions and how to handle them inchapter 27.

When theat()member function encounters an out-of-bounds index, it actually throws an exception of typestd::out_of_range. If the exception is not handled, the program will be terminated. We cover exceptions and how to handle them inchapter 27.

Under the hood,std::vectorholds its elements in a C-style array. Thedata()member function returns a pointer to this underlying C-style array, which we can then index. Since C-style arrays allow indexing with both signed and unsigned types, we don’t run into any sign conversion issues. We discuss C-style arrays further in lessons17.7 -- Introduction to C-style arraysand17.8 -- C-style array decay.
Author’s noteWe’ll discuss additional options to address such indexing challenges in lesson16.7 -- Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions.
We’ll discuss additional options to address such indexing challenges in lesson16.7 -- Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions.

Question #2a) What issize_typeand what is it used for?Show Solutionsize_typeis a nested typedef that is an alias for the type used to store the length (and indices, if supported) of a standard library container.b) What type doessize_typedefault to? Is it signed or unsigned?Show Solutionstd::size_t, which is an unsigned type.c) Which functions to get the length of a container returnsize_type?Show SolutionThesize()member function andstd::sizeboth returnsize_type.Next lesson16.4Passing std::vectorBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson16.2Introduction to std::vector and list constructors
